flow.text: Post-op bacteremia + clean wound + no UTI + no pneumonia → check the nose.
flow.subtext: Don't just swab the wound. The reservoir is elsewhere.
flow.insight: Unexplained post-op staph → assume nasal source

flow.text: Next station: the spleen.
flow.subtext: From colonization to bacteremia. And from bacteremia to — the filter. What happens when blood passes through an organ designed to catch bugs?
flow.insight: Next: spleen filtering + encapsulated organisms

stations.bridge: A woman five days after a hip replacement — fever, but a clean-looking incision, no urinary symptoms, no cough. Blood cultures grow S. aureus. The surgical wound isn't the source. So where is it?
stations.vignette: A 60-year-old woman undergoes an uncomplicated total hip arthroplasty. On postoperative day 5, she develops fever and malaise. The surgical incision appears clean and dry without erythema or drainage. She has no cough, dysuria, or abdominal tenderness. Blood cultures are positive for Staphylococcus aureus.
stations.stem: The source of this patient's bacteremia is most likely colonization at which of the following sites?
keypoints: The anterior nares are the primary reservoir for S. aureus in humans — about 30% of healthy adults are persistent carriers.
In the perioperative setting, bacteria from the nasal reservoir can seed hardware and cause prosthetic joint infection even when the surgical site itself looks clean.
Other sites: GI = E. coli/enterococci · oropharynx = S. pneumoniae/viridans · perineum/UT = gram-negatives or enterococci.

stations.label: Station 02
stations.title: Encapsulated-organism clearance requires the spleen
stations.subtitle: Encapsulated killers.

explanationDiagrams.caption: Top: why capsules block phagocytosis, and how opsonization (antibody + complement) restores the grip. Middle: the five encapsulated organisms. Bottom: what asplenic patients lose.

flow.text: The spleen filters blood for bacteria — especially encapsulated ones.
flow.subtext: Blood passes through splenic sinusoids. Macrophages pluck out bacteria + damaged cells.
flow.insight: Spleen = blood filter + antibody factory

flow.text: Capsule = a sugar cloak that hides PAMPs.
flow.subtext: Phagocytes can't grip the slippery surface. Capsule shields the bug from direct killing.
flow.insight: Capsule blocks phagocytosis — slippery to PMNs

flow.text: Solution: opsonize the capsule with antibody + complement.
flow.subtext: Antibody (IgM/IgG) binds capsule. C3b deposits on surface. Both create handles for phagocytes.
flow.insight: Opsonization = handles on capsule (Ab + C3b)

flow.prompt: Which two splenic functions are critical for handling encapsulated organisms?

flow.text: The spleen does two key things.
flow.subtext: 1) Mechanical filter: bacteria trapped in red pulp, eaten by macrophages. 2) IgM memory B cells: specialized antibody production against encapsulated bugs.
flow.insight: Spleen: (1) filter + (2) IgM memory B cells

flow.text: The 5 key encapsulated organisms.
flow.subtext: S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, N. meningitidis, Klebsiella, Group B Strep. All wear a sugar cape. All need the spleen.
flow.insight: 5 capsule bugs: S.pneumo / H.flu / N.mening / Klebs / GBS

flow.question: After splenectomy, which SINGLE organism is most important to vaccinate against?

feedback.correct: Correct. S. pneumoniae is by far the most common and dangerous organism in asplenic patients. Both PCV13 and PPSV23 are given. H. flu and meningococcal vaccines also indicated.
feedback.wrong: S. aureus and E. coli are common pathogens generally, but neither is the top asplenic threat. The capsule + respiratory transmission of S. pneumoniae makes it the #1 danger.

flow.text: Remove the spleen → both functions vanish.
flow.subtext: No mechanical filter. No IgM memory antibody factory. Encapsulated bugs now have free run.
flow.insight: Splenectomy → both filter & antibody loss

flow.text: Next stop: the smear.
flow.subtext: How can we tell, from a simple blood smear, whether the spleen is working? Howell-Jolly bodies — next station.
flow.insight: Next: blood-smear tell of missing/failing spleen

stations.bridge: A clinical scenario about a patient whose spleen has been removed: what class of organism is now most dangerous?
stations.vignette: A 34-year-old man underwent splenectomy two years ago following a motor vehicle collision that caused splenic rupture. His medical team wants to ensure appropriate long-term prophylaxis and vaccination.
stations.stem: This patient is at greatest risk of overwhelming infection from bacteria with which of the following structural features?
keypoints: The spleen is the primary clearance site for encapsulated organisms: S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae type b, and N. meningitidis.
All three vaccinations are mandatory post-splenectomy.

Acid-fast cell walls = mycobacteria (cell-mediated). Flagella don't confer phagocytic resistance. IgA protease is a virulence factor but isn't the splenic-clearance issue. LPS = gram-negatives, handled by neutrophils.

bank.label: Bonus · Most dangerous organism
bank.vignette: A 40-year-old woman status post splenectomy presents with sudden fever, chills, and hypotension. Blood cultures are pending.
bank.stem: Which organism is most likely responsible for her presentation?
bank.explanation: S. pneumoniae is by far the most common organism in overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI). It causes the majority of fatal cases.

stations.label: Station 03
stations.title: Terminal complement (C5–C9) and Neisseria
stations.subtitle: Three triggers. One kill switch.

explanationDiagrams.caption: Three pathways converge at C3 convertase. C3b opsonizes and drives C5 convertase. The MAC assembles and drills the membrane. Regulators prevent self-damage — and the diseases are the mirror image of which regulator is missing.

flow.text: Complement = ~30 plasma proteins, circulating inactive.
flow.subtext: Not cells. Not antibodies. Proteins in the blood that activate only when triggered.
flow.insight: Complement is plasma proteins — no cells required

flow.text: Three trigger pathways, one convergence point.
flow.subtext: Classical (antibody-antigen) · Lectin (MBL → mannose) · Alternative (spontaneous, amplified on microbial surfaces).
flow.insight: Three pathways → all converge at C3 convertase

flow.text: C3 is split. C3b opsonizes. C3a inflames.
flow.subtext: C3b coats the microbe for phagocytosis and builds the next enzyme (C5 convertase). C3a is an anaphylatoxin.
flow.insight: C3b = opsonin + amplifier · C3a = anaphylatoxin

flow.text: C5b joins C6–C9 = MAC. The pore lyses the microbe.
flow.subtext: Membrane Attack Complex — a ring of proteins drilled through the membrane. Water rushes in. The bacterium explodes.
flow.insight: MAC = C5b + C6 + C7 + C8 + C9 ring → pore → osmotic lysis

flow.text: Regulators keep complement from attacking self.
flow.subtext: C1-INH brakes classical · DAF + CD59 block MAC on your own red cells · Factor H restrains alternative pathway.
flow.insight: Regulators: C1-INH (classical brake), DAF/CD59 (self MAC shield)

flow.text: Every regulator has a disease. Every convertase has one too.
flow.subtext: Deficiencies map directly to presentations — memorize the map and the board questions solve themselves.
flow.insight: C1-INH↓ = hereditary angioedema · C5-C9↓ = Neisseria · DAF/CD59↓ = PNH · C3↓ = pyogenic + IC disease

stations.bridge: A college student has had three separate episodes of bacterial meningitis in two years — all caused by Neisseria meningitidis. Her immune workup shows normal IgG, IgA, and IgM. Her T- and B-cell counts are normal. But her CH50 (total complement) is low. The pathogen and the pattern together point to a specific piece of complement missing.
stations.vignette:
A 20-year-old college student is hospitalized with her third episode of bacterial meningitis in two years. Cerebrospinal fluid cultures from all three episodes have grown Neisseria meningitidis. Between episodes she is healthy and has no other infections of note. Her CBC is normal. Immunoglobulin levels are normal. T-cell and B-cell subset counts are normal. A CH50 assay, which measures total complement activity, is markedly low.

She is otherwise well-appearing with no family history of autoimmune disease.
stations.stem: A deficiency in which of the following best explains this patient's recurrent Neisseria infections?
keypoints: Neisseria species (meningitidis + gonorrhoeae) are uniquely dependent on the membrane attack complex for clearance.
Deficiency in any of C5–C9 blocks MAC assembly → recurrent neisserial infections (the classic board pattern).
Other complement defects: C1-INH = HAE (swelling, not infection) · C3 = pyogenic infections + immune-complex disease · DAF = PNH · MBL = diffuse infections in infancy.
Narrow Neisseria pattern + low CH50 = MAC deficiency. Every time.

stations.label: Station 04
stations.title: NK deficiency → severe herpesvirus infection
stations.subtitle: The missing-self killer.

explanationDiagrams.caption: Healthy cell: lots of MHC-I, inhibitory signal dominates, NK spares it. Infected/tumor cell: MHC-I lost, activating signal wins, NK kills it via perforin + granzyme.

flow.text: The problem: some threats hide inside host cells.
flow.subtext: Viruses replicate inside your own epithelium. Tumor cells look nearly identical to healthy self. Complement and antibodies can't reach them.
flow.insight: Viruses + tumor cells hide inside host cells — unreachable by complement / antibodies

flow.text: The NK cell: large granular lymphocyte. No antibody, no prior exposure needed.
flow.subtext: Part of the innate immune system. Constantly circulates. Checks every cell. Born knowing how to kill.
flow.insight: NK cell = innate cellular killer · no prior exposure required

flow.text: Missing-self hypothesis: healthy cells show MHC-I. Infected / tumor cells often don't.
flow.subtext: Viruses and tumors downregulate MHC-I to hide from CD8 T cells — but then NK cells notice the absence.
flow.insight: MHC-I present = healthy / MHC-I absent = suspicious

flow.text: Two receptors, one decision: inhibitory vs activating.
flow.subtext: KIRs (inhibitory) bind MHC-I → don't kill. NKG2D (activating) binds stress ligands → kill. Strongest signal wins.
flow.insight: Inhibitory (MHC-I) vs Activating (stress ligands) — strongest signal wins

flow.text: Kill mechanism: perforin pore + granzyme → apoptosis.
flow.subtext: Perforin polymerizes a pore. Granzymes enter and activate caspases. Clean, controlled death — no inflammatory spill.
flow.insight: Perforin pore → granzyme entry → caspase activation → apoptosis

flow.text: ADCC: the Fc receptor bridges innate and adaptive.
flow.subtext: CD16 on NK cells binds IgG Fc regions. If a cell is antibody-coated, NK grabs it and kills via the same perforin-granzyme pathway.
flow.insight: ADCC: CD16 binds IgG-coated targets → same perforin/granzyme kill

stations.bridge: A 14-year-old girl has had severe, recurrent herpesvirus infections since infancy — disseminated HSV as an infant, severe VZV as a toddler, CMV mononucleosis in childhood. Her antibody levels are normal. Her T-cell counts are normal. But on flow cytometry, one lymphocyte population is nearly absent. The pattern — bad at herpes, good at everything else — points to the deficit.
stations.vignette:
A 14-year-old girl is referred to immunology after a third severe herpesvirus infection — this time CMV mononucleosis requiring hospitalization. Her history includes disseminated neonatal HSV at 4 weeks of age and severe varicella with pneumonitis at age 3. Between herpesvirus episodes she is well, with no recurrent bacterial infections, no opportunistic fungi, and no autoimmune disease.

Lab studies show normal total IgG, IgA, and IgM. T-cell subset counts (CD3+, CD4+, CD8+) are all within normal range. Flow cytometry for the CD16+/CD56+ lymphocyte population shows this population is nearly absent.
stations.stem: A deficiency in which of the following cell types best explains this patient's pattern of infections?
keypoints: Severe and recurrent herpesvirus infections (HSV, VZV, CMV) with otherwise normal immunity → NK cell deficiency.
NK cells are the primary innate defense against herpesviruses — they kill infected cells via missing-self and ADCC.
Flow cytometry: absent CD16+/CD56+ lymphocytes directly identifies NK cells.
B-cell def = encapsulated bacteria + low Ig. CD4 def = opportunistic (PCP, fungi, atypical mycobacteria). Neutrophil def = pyogenic. pDCs aren't CD16+/CD56+.

stations.label: Station 05
stations.title: Howell-Jolly bodies → functional asplenia
stations.subtitle: Howell-Jolly bodies.

explanationDiagrams.caption: Top: the smear view. Middle: the pitting mechanism that normally removes Howell-Jolly bodies. Below: the two causes of asplenia. The clinical answer chain always lands on S. pneumoniae.

flow.text: RBCs start life nucleated. They extrude the nucleus as they mature.
flow.subtext: Bone marrow erythroid precursors have nuclei. Before leaving marrow, they spit out the nucleus.
flow.insight: Mature RBC = no nucleus, by design

flow.text: Sometimes a small nuclear fragment is left behind.
flow.subtext: Incomplete extrusion. Tiny piece of DNA remains inside. This is a Howell-Jolly body.
flow.insight: Howell-Jolly = leftover DNA fragment in RBC

flow.text: A healthy spleen plucks these fragments out.
flow.subtext: Splenic red pulp macrophages line the sinusoids. As RBCs squeeze through, H-J bodies get pitted out.
flow.insight: Splenic pitting = removal of H-J bodies from RBCs

flow.prompt: You see Howell-Jolly bodies on the smear. What does that TELL you about the patient?

flow.text: H-J bodies on smear → spleen is not filtering.
flow.subtext: Either absent (surgical splenectomy) or not working (functional asplenia). Both equally dangerous.
flow.insight: H-J bodies visible = spleen not doing its job

flow.text: Classic cause: sickle cell disease → autosplenectomy.
flow.subtext: Repeat sickling infarcts the spleen over years. By adolescence, the spleen is a fibrotic scar. Functionally absent.
flow.insight: Sickle cell → autosplenectomy → H-J + encapsulated risk

flow.question: Howell-Jolly bodies on peripheral smear mean the patient is most at risk for —

choices.text: Overwhelming infection with encapsulated bacteria (e.g. S. pneumoniae)

feedback.correct: Right. H-J bodies = functional asplenia = loss of both the filter and IgM memory B cells. Encapsulated organisms — especially S. pneumoniae — are the biggest danger.
feedback.wrong: EBV and intracellular bacteria are controlled by T cells — not spleen-dependent. The asplenic vulnerability is specifically to ENCAPSULATED organisms handled by Ab + complement.

flow.text: Other causes: surgical splenectomy, splenic infarct, cirrhosis with congestion.
flow.subtext: Any process that stops macrophages from pitting DNA fragments will cause H-J bodies to appear.
flow.insight: Causes: sickle cell, splenectomy, infarct, cirrhosis

flow.text: Next station: the tempo of infection in the asplenic patient.
flow.subtext: No filter. No specific antibody. How fast can things fall apart? Hours.
flow.insight: Next: OPSI — 12 hours from 'feeling off' to septic shock

stations.bridge: A child with sickle cell disease. His smear shows Howell-Jolly bodies. Which infection is he most at risk for?
stations.vignette: A 7-year-old boy with homozygous sickle cell disease is evaluated during a well-child visit. He has been maintained on hydroxyurea and penicillin prophylaxis since infancy. Peripheral blood smear reveals sickled erythrocytes along with round, basophilic inclusions within otherwise normal-appearing red blood cells.
stations.stem: The finding described on peripheral blood smear places this patient at greatest risk for infection with which of the following organisms?
keypoints: Howell-Jolly bodies = functional asplenia — here from chronic autoinfarction in sickle cell disease.
Greatest risk = encapsulated organisms; S. pneumoniae leads as the top cause of life-threatening infection in this group.
CMV and Pneumocystis are T-cell-deficiency concerns. TB needs cell-mediated immunity. Pseudomonas is a neutropenia concern.

stations.label: Station 06
stations.title: Post-splenectomy sepsis (OPSI)
stations.subtitle: OPSI.

explanationDiagrams.caption: The crash is fast because two defenses are missing at once: IgM memory B cell antibody and splenic macrophage filtration. Bacteria multiply in blood before the host can respond.

flow.text: A minor trigger: URI, cold, sinus infection.
flow.subtext: Something seeds nasopharyngeal bacteria into blood. Normally no big deal. In asplenia, catastrophic.
flow.insight: OPSI trigger: minor colonization event / URI

flow.text: Normally, the spleen catches bacteria in seconds.
flow.subtext: Every heartbeat sends blood through splenic sinusoids. Encapsulated bugs get opsonized and removed.
flow.insight: Normal spleen: bacteremia → cleared in minutes

flow.text: No spleen → bacteria double every ~30 minutes.
flow.subtext: No filter removing them. No targeted IgM. Bacteremia is exponential.
flow.insight: Without spleen: bacteria grow exponentially in blood

flow.prompt: What's the final common pathway that makes OPSI lethal?

flow.text: Massive bacteremia → cytokine storm → sepsis.
flow.subtext: TNF, IL-1, IL-6 flood the body. Vasodilation. Shock. Endothelium activated everywhere.
flow.insight: Cytokine storm → systemic sepsis

flow.text: Endothelium activated everywhere → DIC.
flow.subtext: Disseminated clotting in small vessels. Consumes platelets + clotting factors. Simultaneous thrombosis AND bleeding.
flow.insight: DIC: clots + bleeds in parallel

flow.question: The asplenic patient with sepsis has a rapidly falling platelet count and new oozing from IV sites. Why both at once?

choices.text: DIC: consumption of platelets and clotting factors by widespread microthrombi

feedback.correct: Exactly. Endothelial damage triggers the coagulation cascade system-wide. Platelets and fibrinogen get consumed in microthrombi, then the patient bleeds from whatever site is already open.
feedback.wrong: HIT and autoimmune thrombocytopenia don't cause active bleeding alongside thrombosis like DIC does. Sepsis + asplenia = consumptive coagulopathy is the classic mechanism.

flow.text: 12 hours from feeling-off to septic shock.
flow.subtext: Antibiotics given within the first few hours change outcomes. Given late, often don't.
flow.insight: Clinical window: ~hours, not days. Treat early.

flow.text: Next: the lifelong plan for an asplenic patient.
flow.subtext: How do we prevent OPSI in the first place? Vaccines. Prophylaxis. Education.
flow.insight: Next: multi-decade asplenic care plan

stations.bridge: An asplenic young woman crashing with sepsis and DIC. What explains the speed?
stations.vignette: A 29-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with fever, chills, and confusion that began 6 hours ago. She had a splenectomy 6 years earlier after a motor vehicle collision. BP is 80/48 mm Hg. Labs show platelets 42,000/μL, prolonged PT and aPTT, and elevated D-dimer. Blood cultures are drawn.
stations.stem: Which of the following best explains this patient's rapid clinical deterioration?

choices.text: Absence of IgM memory B cells and splenic clearance of encapsulated bacteria
Impaired T-cell activation against intracellular pathogens
Reduced complement activity from autoimmune destruction

keypoints: OPSI is driven by loss of two synergistic splenic functions: (1) mechanical filtering of opsonized bacteria from blood, and (2) the IgM memory B-cell response that produces early protective antibody against encapsulated organisms.
Without both, pneumococcus entering the bloodstream multiplies unchecked — hence the 12-hour sepsis timeline.
Splenectomy normally causes thrombocytosis, not -penia. Hypogammaglobulinemia, T-cell defects, and complement issues aren't post-splenectomy effects.

bank.label: Bonus · The prophylactic antibiotic
bank.vignette: A patient is counseled that after splenectomy she should keep amoxicillin-clavulanate available and take it at the first sign of fever, even before evaluation.
bank.stem: The rationale for this self-administered antibiotic is best explained by which principle?

choices.text: Encapsulated bacteria resist phagocytosis unless pre-opsonized
Gram-negative rods require early carbapenem coverage
Without a spleen, complement is inactivated

bank.explanation: The rationale is that splenic function is needed to clear encapsulated organisms, and once bacteremia begins, time-to-antibiotic is the most important predictor of survival.

stations.label: Station 07
stations.title: Vaccination + prophylaxis in asplenic children
stations.subtitle: Lifelong risk.

explanationDiagrams.caption: Age-based immune maturation, the 4-step lifelong plan set against a decades timeline, and the physiology of platelet sequestration.

flow.text: Children under ~5 have immature adaptive immunity.
flow.subtext: They rely more heavily on spleen for encapsulated defense. Remove the spleen in a young child → highest-risk window.
flow.insight: Young kids = most dependent on splenic function

flow.text: Plan #1: vaccinate against the 3 big encapsulated bugs.
flow.subtext: Pneumococcal (PCV13 + PPSV23). H. influenzae type b. Meningococcal (MenACWY + MenB).
flow.insight: Asplenic vaccine trio: pneumo + Hib + meningo

flow.text: Plan #2: daily prophylactic penicillin (at least through age 5).
flow.subtext: Oral penicillin VK daily. Some centers continue longer in high-risk patients.
flow.insight: Daily PO penicillin through early childhood

flow.prompt: The child still goes out in the world — to school, on trips. How do the parents manage a sudden fever?

flow.text: Plan #3: emergency antibiotics on hand.
flow.subtext: Parents carry a dose of oral amoxicillin-clavulanate (or similar). ANY fever → take dose AND go straight to ER.
flow.insight: Stand-by antibiotics at home + immediate ER

flow.text: Plan #4: educate parents that fever = EMERGENCY.
flow.subtext: Not a wait-and-see. Not a let’s-see-if-it-resolves. The window between URI-like symptoms and septic shock is hours.
flow.insight: Asplenic fever = no wait-and-see

flow.question: Side effect of splenectomy to explain to parents: which way does the platelet count go?

choices.text: Up (thrombocytosis — spleen normally sequesters ~1/3 of platelets)

feedback.correct: Right. The spleen normally holds about a third of circulating platelets. Remove it, and they all go to the bloodstream — expect thrombocytosis.
feedback.wrong: Trick question. Splenectomy causes thrombocytOSIS (high) because the spleen normally sequesters platelets. A common distractor on exams.

flow.text: Side effect: thrombocytosis, not thrombocytopenia.
flow.subtext: Spleen sequesters ~1/3 of platelets. Remove it → platelets all released to circulation. Levels rise, sometimes markedly.
flow.insight: Splenectomy → HIGH platelets (not low)

flow.text: Next: neutropenia — a totally different kind of immunodeficiency.
flow.subtext: Spleen problems = antibody/complement vulnerability. Neutrophil problems = different bugs, different fears.
flow.insight: Next: what goes wrong when NEUTROPHILS disappear

stations.bridge: A pediatric follow-up visit. The right long-term complication to emphasize to her parents is…
stations.vignette: A 5-year-old girl underwent splenectomy 6 months ago following a traumatic abdominal injury from a bicycle accident. She has recovered well and has no complaints today. Her parents ask what they should monitor for in the long term.
stations.stem: Which of the following is the most important long-term complication to discuss with her parents?
keypoints: Lifelong asplenia = lifelong risk of OPSI with encapsulated organisms, especially S. pneumoniae.
Children under 5 are highest-risk because of immature humoral immunity → vaccination + prophylactic antibiotics are essential.
Lymphopenia, CMV, lymphoma, and thrombocytopenia aren't asplenia-specific concerns. (Splenectomy actually causes thrombocytosis.)

stations.label: Station 08
stations.title: Pseudomonas as first-line empiric coverage
stations.subtitle: Febrile neutropenia.

explanationDiagrams.caption: Drug → marrow → blood → tissue. Each scene shows the causal step. Top row: the healthy neutrophil supply chain. Bottom row: what chemotherapy breaks.

flow.text: Chemotherapy kills rapidly dividing cells.
flow.subtext: Tumor cells divide fast. So do marrow precursors, gut epithelium, hair follicles. Collateral damage across the board.
flow.insight: Chemo kills all fast-dividing cells (not just tumor)

flow.text: The bone marrow is hit hard.
flow.subtext: All 3 lineages suppressed: RBCs, platelets, neutrophils. But neutrophils have the shortest half-life (~6-8h) — they crash first.
flow.insight: Neutrophils crash fastest (short half-life)

flow.text: ANC drops to near zero by day 7-10.
flow.subtext: Absolute Neutrophil Count < 500/μL = severe neutropenia. < 100/μL = profound. No cells = no pus = atypical presentations.
flow.insight: ANC < 500 = severe; ANC < 100 = profound

flow.prompt: A neutropenic patient spikes a fever. Why is this an emergency, when a fever in a healthy person is usually not?

flow.text: No neutrophils → gut/skin flora invade unopposed.
flow.subtext: Gut bacteria translocate across damaged mucosa. Skin flora enter through IV lines. No PMNs to stop them.
flow.insight: Neutropenic fever → flora translocation / line infection

flow.text: Classic organism: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (gram-negative rod).
flow.subtext: Plus gram-positives from central line (coag-neg staph, S. aureus). Plus gut E. coli/Klebsiella. Plus fungi (Candida) after prolonged courses.
flow.insight: Febrile neutropenia bugs: Pseudomonas, gram+ from lines, gut Gram-

flow.question: A man with ANC of 50 after chemo spikes a fever. A gram-negative rod grows in blood cultures. Most likely organism?

feedback.correct: Exactly. Gram-negative rod in the febrile neutropenic patient = Pseudomonas classically. Empirical antipseudomonal coverage (cefepime, pip-tazo, or carbapenem) is started before cultures return.
feedback.wrong: S. pneumoniae is the asplenia classic (gram-positive diplococci, not GNR). TB doesn't typically present as acute fever in a neutropenic patient. GNR + chemo fever = think Pseudomonas first.

flow.text: Treatment: broad-spectrum antipseudomonal ASAP.
flow.subtext: Cefepime, piperacillin-tazobactam, or carbapenem. Don't wait for cultures. Start within an hour of presentation.
flow.insight: Empirical anti-Pseudomonal antibiotics within 1 hour

flow.text: Next: what if neutropenia is congenital, not iatrogenic?
flow.subtext: An infant with isolated low ANC. Same vulnerability, different workup. Pattern recognition time.
flow.insight: Next: congenital isolated neutropenia in an infant

stations.bridge: A man with zero neutrophils after chemo spikes a fever and grows a gram-negative rod. Which organism fits this pattern?
stations.vignette: A 59-year-old man with acute myelogenous leukemia is day 8 of induction chemotherapy. He develops fever to 39.1°C and chills but has no localizing symptoms and a normal chest examination. His total WBC is 200/μL with an absolute neutrophil count of 50/μL. Blood cultures drawn on admission grow a gram-negative rod.
stations.stem: The most likely causative organism is which of the following?
keypoints: Profound neutropenia creates specific vulnerability to gram-negative rods, with Pseudomonas aeruginosa as the classic and most feared pathogen in febrile neutropenia.
Empiric anti-pseudomonal therapy (cefepime, pip-tazo, meropenem) starts before cultures return.
E. coli is possible but less specific. H. influenzae & N. meningitidis are encapsulated (asplenia/humoral territory). S. pneumoniae is gram-positive.

stations.label: Station 09
stations.title: Cell-line half-life → which drops first
stations.subtitle: Congenital neutropenia.

explanationDiagrams.caption: The hematopoietic lineage tree and three patterns of failure at different anatomic levels. The pattern of the CBC tells you which compartment is broken.

flow.text: When a patient has infections, first ask: what's low on the CBC?
flow.subtext: The pattern of the cytopenia narrows the differential before you even know the mutation.
flow.insight: Pattern-matching: which cell line is missing?

flow.text: Everything low → stem cell / marrow failure.
flow.subtext: Pancytopenia: RBCs, platelets, neutrophils all down. Think aplastic anemia, myelodysplasia, or marrow replacement.
flow.insight: Pancytopenia → stem cell / marrow problem

flow.text: Only lymphocytes low → lymphoid lineage problem.
flow.subtext: Selective lymphopenia. Think SCID in infants, HIV, congenital combined immunodeficiencies.
flow.insight: Isolated lymphopenia → lymphoid-specific defect

flow.prompt: If just the neutrophils are low (and everything else is normal), where is the defect?

flow.text: Only neutrophils low → neutrophil lineage-specific defect.
flow.subtext: Myeloid progenitor problem affecting only the granulocyte line. Or circulation / destruction issue at the mature level.
flow.insight: Isolated neutropenia → myeloid lineage defect

flow.text: Classic cause in an infant: Kostmann syndrome (severe congenital neutropenia).
flow.subtext: Autosomal recessive. ELANE or HAX1 mutations. ANC typically < 200/μL from birth. Recurrent severe bacterial infections. Cyclic neutropenia is another option (21-day cycles).
flow.insight: Kostmann (ELANE/HAX1) = congenital severe neutropenia

flow.question: A 4-month-old with recurrent skin abscesses, oral ulcers, and ANC of 150. Hgb and platelets normal. Best dx?

feedback.correct: Yes. Isolated neutropenia (everything else normal) + infant + recurrent bacterial = Kostmann / severe congenital neutropenia. Treat with G-CSF.
feedback.wrong: SCID affects LYMPHOCYTES specifically (would show T/B cell deficits). Aplastic anemia causes PANCYTOPENIA (all lines low). Isolated neutropenia in an infant points squarely at Kostmann.

flow.text: Clinical pattern: recurrent bacterial + fungal from birth.
flow.subtext: Skin abscesses, oral ulcers, perirectal infections, pneumonia. Staph aureus and gram-negative rods common. Fungi later.
flow.insight: Kostmann: bacterial + fungal infections from infancy

flow.text: You've closed the systemic story. Next chapter: the long siege.
flow.subtext: Chapter 4 shifts from neutrophils to macrophages and T cells. Tuberculosis. Granulomas. Chronic infections that last years, not hours.
flow.insight: Next chapter: macrophage + T cell battles (TB, granulomas)

stations.bridge: An infant with isolated low ANC and no other abnormalities — which diagnosis fits best?
stations.vignette: A 6-week-old girl is evaluated after routine bloodwork shows a low white blood cell count. She was born at term after an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery. She is feeding and growing well and has had no fevers or infections. Physical exam is normal. Complete blood count shows hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL, platelets 312,000/μL, lymphocytes within normal range, and absolute neutrophil count of 600/μL (low). Repeat testing over several weeks confirms persistent isolated neutropenia.
stations.stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.text: Alloimmune hemolytic disease of the newborn

keypoints: Pattern: isolated neutropenia from birth + normal Hb, platelets, and lymphocytes + no recurrent infections = congenital neutropenia.
Distinguishers: alloimmune hemolytic disease causes anemia. Congenital CMV → microcephaly + hearing loss + petechiae + jaundice.
DiGeorge = T-cell deficiency + cardiac defects + hypocalcemia. SCID = combined T+B deficiency with severe infections — not this clinical picture.

chapters.title: Chronic inflammation & granulomas
chapters.subtitle: When the quick response fails, the body settles in — macrophages, T-cell help, granulomas, and the architecture of chronic containment.

stations.label: Station 01
stations.title: IFN-γ receptor defect → disseminated mycobacteria
stations.subtitle: IFN-γ activation.

explanationDiagrams.caption: The immunological synapse. Upper: MHC II docks with TCR, IL-12 crosses the cleft. Lower: Th1 sends IFN-γ back, binds IFN-γ receptor on the macrophage. Break any piece → MSMD.

flow.text: The macrophage eats the mycobacterium, but can't kill it.
flow.subtext: Phagocytosis works fine. Killing fails. Mycobacterial cell wall lipids block phagolysosome acidification.
flow.insight: Mycobacteria survive INSIDE macrophages

flow.text: Macrophage calls for help: displays antigen on MHC II, pumps out IL-12.
flow.subtext: Mycobacterial peptide fragments get loaded onto MHC II on macrophage surface. IL-12 is the recruiting cytokine.
flow.insight: Macrophage signals: MHC II (display) + IL-12 (recruit)

flow.text: A Th1 cell reads the message via TCR.
flow.subtext: CD4+ T cell binds the macrophage's MHC II / peptide complex. IL-12 pushes it toward Th1 phenotype.
flow.insight: Th1 = CD4+ T cell polarized by IL-12 on macrophage

flow.prompt: Th1 now has to tell the macrophage to KILL. What signal does it send back?

flow.text: Th1 fires IFN-γ back at the macrophage.
flow.subtext: Interferon-gamma binds IFN-γ receptor on macrophage. Triggers transcription of antimicrobial genes.
flow.insight: IFN-γ = the kill-now signal to macrophage

flow.text: Activated macrophage: phagolysosome maturation + ROS surge = KILL.
flow.subtext: Phagolysosome finally acidifies. NADPH oxidase reassembles. Nitric oxide produced. Mycobacterium dies.
flow.insight: Activation lets phagolysosome fuse + ROS fire

flow.question: A child has disseminated atypical mycobacteria. Immunoglobulins normal. T-cell counts normal. Where's the defect?

feedback.correct: Exactly. Mendelian Susceptibility to Mycobacterial Disease (MSMD). Mutations in IL-12, IL-12R, IFN-γ, or IFN-γR. T cells are there, antibodies are there — but the macrophage-T cell conversation is broken.
feedback.wrong: With normal Ig and normal T-cell counts, the defect isn't in B cells or complement. Disseminated atypical mycobacteria specifically point to the macrophage-activation axis (IL-12/IFN-γ).

flow.text: Break any piece → MSMD (disseminated mycobacterial infection).
flow.subtext: IL-12 mutation. IL-12R mutation. IFN-γ mutation. IFN-γR mutation. Any break stops the loop.
flow.insight: MSMD = break in IL-12 ↔ Th1 ↔ IFN-γ loop

flow.text: Next: what does a chronically activated macrophage build?
flow.subtext: When killing keeps failing, macrophages wall off the infection. A structure emerges — the granuloma.
flow.insight: Next: pyogenic (PMN) vs granulomatous (macrophage) pattern

stations.bridge: A child with recurrent disseminated atypical mycobacterial infections. Immunoglobulins are normal, T-cell counts are normal. Where is the defect?
stations.vignette: A 9-year-old boy with a history of disseminated Mycobacterium avium complex infection is evaluated for recurrent fevers and weight loss. His quantitative immunoglobulins, CD4 and CD8 T-cell counts, and NK cell numbers are all normal. Whole exome sequencing reveals a mutation in a gene encoding a cytokine receptor on macrophages.
stations.stem: Which receptor is most likely defective in this patient?
keypoints: Disseminated atypical mycobacterial infection + normal T-cell and B-cell numbers = Mendelian susceptibility to mycobacterial disease.
Most common defect: IFN-γ receptor. Without IFN-γ signaling, macrophages can't be activated to kill intracellular mycobacteria.
CD40L defect = hyper-IgM. IL-2R = SCID. TLR4 = LPS recognition. TNFR = different phenotypes entirely.

stations.label: Station 02
stations.title: Neutrophil-dominant acute inflammation
stations.subtitle: Pyogenic pattern.

explanationDiagrams.caption: Two biopsy fields side-by-side. Left: neutrophil-packed pus center with acid hydrolases liquefying tissue. Right: organized ring of macrophages + giant cell + lymphocyte rim walling off intracellular pathogens.

flow.text: Two patterns of chronic lesion: pyogenic vs granulomatous.
flow.subtext: A biopsy tells you which one. The cellular cast is diagnostic.
flow.insight: Biopsy cell type = which immune arm is active

flow.text: Pyogenic: center = sea of neutrophils, acid hydrolases, liquefaction.
flow.subtext: Extracellular bacteria (S. aureus, Strep, gram-neg rods). PMNs pour in, rupture, release enzymes. Tissue dissolves. Pus fills the cavity.
flow.insight: Pyogenic = PMN pus + liquefactive necrosis

flow.text: Granulomatous: organized ring of macrophages, multinucleate giant cells, lymphocyte rim.
flow.subtext: For intracellular bugs (TB, fungi, Brucella) that hide inside macrophages. Walls them off rather than liquefying.
flow.insight: Granuloma = organized cellular wall around intracellular bug

flow.prompt: Why do intracellular pathogens elicit granulomas instead of pus?

flow.text: Intracellular bugs can't be killed directly — they must be walled off.
flow.subtext: PMN enzymes don't reach bugs hiding inside macrophages. Answer: a cellular cage. Th1 recruits more macrophages that fuse into giant cells.
flow.insight: No direct kill → surround + contain = granuloma

flow.text: Caseating granulomas: center turns cheese-like — the TB signature.
flow.subtext: TB granulomas develop central caseous necrosis. Soft, white, cheese-like. Mycobacterial lipids cause this specific pattern.
flow.insight: Caseous = cheese-like necrosis = TB classic

flow.question: You biopsy a cavitary lung lesion. It's filled with neutrophils and liquefying tissue. Most likely organism class?

feedback.correct: Right. PMN-dominated liquefactive necrosis = pyogenic bacteria. S. aureus is the classic cavitary pneumonia causer (also Klebsiella in alcoholics). TB/fungi would show granulomas, not pus.
feedback.wrong: TB and fungi cause granulomatous inflammation (macrophages, giant cells, possibly caseous necrosis) — not neutrophilic pus. Liquefaction + PMN = extracellular pyogenic.

flow.text: Bug class determines the biopsy pattern.
flow.subtext: Extracellular pyogenic → PMNs + pus. Intracellular (TB, fungi, Brucella) → granuloma + possibly caseation.
flow.insight: Pattern on slide = bug class + right Abx

flow.text: Next: granulomas without any bug at all.
flow.subtext: The Th1 loop can fire in the absence of infection. A disease where granulomas appear in the lungs of a young woman — with no organism to wall off.
flow.insight: Next: sarcoidosis — sterile noncaseating granulomas

stations.bridge: A man with a cavitary lung lesion. You biopsy the center. Which cell type and enzyme class dominate?
stations.vignette: A 65-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history presents with three weeks of productive cough, fever, and right-sided pleuritic chest pain. Chest CT reveals a thick-walled cavitary lesion with an air-fluid level in the right upper lobe. Aspiration of the cavity yields thick, foul-smelling, yellow-green purulent material that is sent for culture and histologic examination.
stations.stem: Histologic examination of this aspirate is most likely to show abundant neutrophils releasing which of the following?
keypoints: The center of a pyogenic abscess = dying neutrophils releasing acid hydrolases — lysosomal enzymes that work at acidic pH.
These enzymes both kill bacteria and liquefy tissue — that's why the abscess center turns soft and fluid-filled.
Bradykinin drives the vascular pain response, not tissue liquefaction. IgG = B-cell derived. IFN-γ = T cells. TGF-β = fibrosis and repair.

stations.label: Station 03
stations.title: Noncaseating granulomas + hilar adenopathy (sarcoidosis)
stations.subtitle: Sarcoidosis.

explanationDiagrams.caption: The full sarcoidosis picture — the chronic Th1/macrophage loop that never resolves, the multi-system organ distribution, the classic vignette, and the iconic bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy on CXR.

flow.text: Sarcoidosis = sterile, noncaseating granulomas in multiple organs.
flow.subtext: No bug. No caseation. Just the Th1/macrophage loop firing without off-switch.
flow.insight: Sarcoid = sterile noncaseating granuloma disease

flow.text: Classic patient: young African-American woman, 20-40.
flow.subtext: Most common demographic by far. Presents with dry cough, fatigue, sometimes rash (erythema nodosum) or eye symptoms (uveitis).
flow.insight: Classic sarcoid demographic: Black women 20-40

flow.text: Iconic finding: bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy on CXR.
flow.subtext: Both hilar regions enlarged symmetrically. Hallmark of sarcoid. Often the first clue.
flow.insight: Bilateral hilar LAD = sarcoid signature

flow.prompt: What's driving the granulomas if there's no bug? Trace the loop from macrophage to T cell and back.

flow.text: The Th1 loop runs without an antigen to clear.
flow.subtext: IL-12 from macrophages ↔ IFN-γ from Th1 cells. The loop keeps cycling. Macrophages keep forming granulomas. Trigger unknown.
flow.insight: Self-sustaining Th1/macrophage cycle = sarcoid pathophys

flow.text: Labs: high ACE, hypercalcemia, elevated 1,25-vitamin D.
flow.subtext: Activated macrophages express 1-α-hydroxylase unregulated → active vit D → calcium uptake ↑. ACE comes from granulomas themselves.
flow.insight: Sarcoid labs: ACE ↑, Ca²⁺ ↑, 1,25-D ↑

flow.question: A young Black woman with dry cough, bilateral hilar LAD, and noncaseating granulomas on biopsy. Diagnosis?

feedback.correct: Yes. Classic sarcoid triad: young Black woman + bilateral hilar LAD + NONcaseating granulomas. Confirmed by biopsy with no organism. Often treated with steroids if symptomatic.
feedback.wrong: TB causes CASEATING granulomas and is typically unilateral/apical. Bacterial pneumonia causes PMN-filled consolidation, not granulomas. NONcaseating + bilateral hilar = sarcoid.

flow.text: Multi-organ: lungs, skin, eyes, heart, and more.
flow.subtext: Sarcoid granulomas can appear anywhere — skin (lupus pernio), eyes (uveitis), heart (arrhythmia), CNS, joints. Lung is most common (>90%).
flow.insight: Sarcoid is a multi-system disease — lung #1

flow.text: Next: granulomas with star-shaped centers.
flow.subtext: What if the granuloma has a bizarre shape — stellate neutrophil clusters walled by macrophages? Cat-scratch disease next.
flow.insight: Next: stellate microabscess — cat-scratch / Bartonella

stations.bridge: A young woman with bilateral hilar adenopathy. The biopsy shows what's described below. What's the diagnosis?
stations.vignette: A 30-year-old woman presents with several months of dry cough, fatigue, and reddish-brown nodular lesions on her cheeks and nose. Chest imaging shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy without parenchymal consolidation. A biopsy of a hilar node shows well-formed granulomas composed of epithelioid histiocytes and multinucleated giant cells without central caseation or acid-fast bacilli.
stations.stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
keypoints: Sarcoidosis is the classic cause of noncaseating granulomas in bilateral hilar lymph nodes in a young adult, often with skin involvement.
Biopsy: epithelioid histiocytes + multinucleated giant cells, no caseation, no organisms.
Cat scratch = stellate microabscesses (next station). TB = caseating, often AFB+. Histoplasmosis = caseating, fungi on special stains. GPA = vasculitis with necrotizing granulomas.

stations.label: Station 04
stations.title: Stellate microabscess → cat-scratch disease
stations.subtitle: Bartonella.

explanationDiagrams.caption: Four granuloma fingerprints side-by-side, then the immune story behind the stellate microabscess: cat scratch → axillary LN → star of neutrophils walled off by macrophages = the hybrid pattern of Bartonella henselae.

flow.text: Each granulomatous infection has a signature pattern.
flow.subtext: TB = caseating. Sarcoid = noncaseating. Bartonella = star-shaped central PMN cluster walled by macrophages.
flow.insight: Granuloma architecture is diagnostic

flow.text: The setup: kitten scratch or bite, 1-2 weeks earlier.
flow.subtext: Bartonella henselae is carried by cats (especially kittens). Inoculation at scratch site. The organism travels via lymphatics.
flow.insight: Cat scratch → Bartonella → regional lymph node

flow.text: 1-2 weeks later: tender regional lymph node.
flow.subtext: Axillary node if scratched on the arm. Inguinal if on the leg. Cervical if on the face. The drainage path tells you which node.
flow.insight: Draining regional node → enlarged + tender

flow.prompt: Biopsy of the node shows a star-shaped center. What's in the star?

flow.text: The star = necrotic neutrophil cluster.
flow.subtext: Neutrophils rush to the organism, die, form a radiating star pattern. Classic stellate microabscess.
flow.insight: Stellate microabscess = dying PMN star

flow.text: Ring of macrophages walls off the star.
flow.subtext: Classic granulomatous wall surrounds the necrotic PMN core. Hybrid pattern: pyogenic center + granulomatous rim.
flow.insight: Bartonella granuloma = PMN star + macrophage wall

flow.question: A young woman has tender axillary adenopathy 2 weeks after a kitten scratch. Biopsy: stellate microabscess walled by macrophages. Organism?

feedback.correct: Yes. Cat-scratch disease. Bartonella henselae. Stellate microabscess + cat exposure + regional tender LAD is nearly pathognomonic.
feedback.wrong: TB causes CASEATING granulomas without stellate microabscesses. S. aureus would cause acute pyogenic LAD, not a granulomatous reaction. Cat scratch + stellate pattern = Bartonella.

flow.text: Lymphogranuloma venereum (Chlamydia trachomatis L1-L3) makes the same pattern.
flow.subtext: Also stellate microabscesses, but in inguinal nodes after a sexual exposure. Same pattern, different bug, different drainage.
flow.insight: Stellate microabscess in inguinal node → think LGV

flow.text: Last station: what happens to T-cell immunity as we age?
flow.subtext: We've seen macrophage-T cell partnerships in action. What happens when the T cell workforce itself starts to shrink?
flow.insight: Next: thymic involution — aging and T-cell decline

stations.bridge: A young woman with tender axillary adenopathy days after a kitten scratch. The biopsy description below is nearly pathognomonic.
stations.vignette: A 22-year-old veterinary technician presents with a tender, enlarged right axillary lymph node that has been growing over 10 days. She recalls being scratched on the right forearm by a stray kitten about two weeks ago; the scratch healed with a small papule. Excisional biopsy of the axillary node demonstrates multiple granulomas, each containing a central star-shaped collection of neutrophils surrounded by a rim of epithelioid histiocytes.
stations.stem: The organism most likely responsible for this histologic pattern is which of the following?
keypoints: Granulomas with stellate microabscesses are nearly pathognomonic for cat-scratch disease, caused by Bartonella henselae.
History of a kitten scratch followed by regional lymphadenitis clinches the diagnosis.
Distinguishers: TB = caseating granulomas · Pasteurella = rapid post-bite cellulitis · Sporothrix = lymphocutaneous nodules · Toxoplasma = reactive lymphadenopathy without stellate microabscesses.

stations.label: Station 05
stations.title: Age-related T-cell decline vs intact antibodies
stations.subtitle: Thymic involution.

explanationDiagrams.caption: Who handles what · how the thymus changes with age (active tissue replaced by fat) · the Chlamydophila experiment · the classic elderly reactivations (shingles, TB).

flow.text: Two arms of adaptive immunity: antibodies (B cells) and T cells.
flow.subtext: Antibodies = extracellular bugs + toxins. T cells = intracellular bugs + viruses + tumor cells. Different jobs.
flow.insight: Ab handles outside; T cells handle inside

flow.text: T cells are educated in the thymus.
flow.subtext: The thymus is the T-cell training school: positive selection (useful TCRs) and negative selection (auto-reactive clones deleted).
flow.insight: Thymus = T cell school (positive + negative selection)

flow.text: With age, the thymus involutes: active tissue → fat.
flow.subtext: Peaks at puberty, shrinks dramatically after. By age 70, most of what was thymus is replaced by adipose.
flow.insight: Aging → thymic tissue replaced by fat

flow.prompt: The thymus is barely functional by age 70. What part of immunity is MOST affected?

flow.text: Result: T-cell immunity declines with age.
flow.subtext: Fewer naive T cells. Narrower TCR repertoire. Reduced Th1 support for macrophages. Antibody levels stay relatively intact.
flow.insight: Aging: T cells decline, antibodies relatively preserved

flow.text: Elderly reactivations: shingles, TB.
flow.subtext: Zoster (VZV) reactivates when T-cell surveillance wanes. Latent TB can reactivate decades later. Intracellular bugs exploit the T-cell gap.
flow.insight: Aging → reactivation of latent intracellular bugs

flow.question: Aged mice fail to clear intracellular Chlamydophila. Antibody levels normal. Which compartment has failed?

feedback.correct: Correct. Intracellular pathogens need T cell / macrophage control. Normal antibodies rules out B cell failure. Age-associated T-cell decline explains the phenotype — same mechanism as shingles/TB reactivation in elderly humans.
feedback.wrong: Normal antibody levels rules out B cell failure. Complement primarily handles extracellular bugs and capsules. Intracellular pathogen + age + normal Ig = T cell / thymic involution.

flow.text: Same principle in humans: elderly need extra protection against intracellular threats.
flow.subtext: Zoster vaccine (Shingrix) recommended ≥ 50. Annual flu shot. Pneumococcal (even though encapsulated, pneumo defense is partly T-dependent).
flow.insight: Age-based vaccines target T cell-controlled pathogens

flow.text: You've laid the foundation of immunology.
flow.subtext: From the first PMN sliding down a vessel, to the macrophage-Th1 conversation, to the granulomas of chronic disease, to how the whole system ages. The innate engine is yours.
flow.insight: Module 1 complete — innate immunity foundation built

stations.bridge: A research finding: aged mice clear intracellular Chlamydophila poorly. Their antibody levels are normal. What's the failing compartment?
stations.vignette: In a laboratory study, young adult and aged mice are both inoculated with Chlamydophila pneumoniae, an obligate intracellular bacterium. Antibody titers against the organism are measured and are equivalent in both groups. However, the aged mice exhibit significantly delayed bacterial clearance from their tissues compared to the young adult mice.
stations.stem: The impaired clearance in aged mice is most likely due to decline in which of the following?
keypoints: Intracellular bacteria like Chlamydophila are cleared primarily by T-cell-mediated immunity — CD4+ Th1 cells activate macrophages via IFN-γ, and CD8+ cytotoxic T cells kill infected cells.
With aging, thymic involution reduces the output of naïve T cells, weakening this arm of immunity while antibody responses remain relatively preserved.
B-cell class switching and complement support humoral immunity (intact in this stem). NK and neutrophils play limited roles against obligate intracellular bacteria.
